segments.s635-g81: ببودند is an older/poetic past plural form meaning “they were” or “they stayed.” یک هفته is a time phrase “one week.” با + noun (می‌به دست) is a prepositional phrase meaning “with … in hand”; می‌به is a poetic compound referring to wine (often quince-flavored wine), and دست here means “hand” (so “with wine in hand”). The whole segment therefore means they spent/ remained for a week with wine in hand.
segments.s635-g82: همه is the subject “everyone/all.” شاد و خرم are coordinated adjectives “happy and joyful,” where و simply links them. به جای نشست is a verb phrase: به indicates motion/tendency “to/into,” جای means “place/seat,” and نشست (past of نشستن) means “sat (down)/took their seats.” Together: “everyone sat down happy and joyful in their places.”
segments.s635-g83: This is a noun phrase identifying participants: سپینود is a personal name. با is the preposition “with.” شاه بهرام گور is the title plus name “King Bahram Gūr” (گور here is part of the epithet). Grammatically this is the prepositional/nominal foreground for the following clause.
segments.s635-g84: چو is a conjunction here meaning “when.” می‌بود is a past/poetic copula meaning “was” (imperfect/past sense). روشن is the predicate adjective “bright/gleaming.” به جام بلور is a locative/qualifying phrase: به indicating into/in relation to the cup, جام “cup,” بلور “crystal.” Together: “when the wine was bright in crystal cups.”
segments.s635-g73: بیاورد is a past tense/preterite verb (poetic form of آورد) meaning 'brought' and appears before its object here (verb–object order allowed in poetry). یاران is the direct object 'companions' and بهرام with the enclitic را marks the possessor/definite phrase 'Bahram’s (companions)' — را is the definite/object marker attached to the noun phrase.
segments.s635-g74: This noun phrase further describes those companions: سواران 'riders' is modified by بازیب, a poetic/participial adjective 'recovered/returned' (i.e., retrieved or come back). The conjunction و links the following prepositional phrase با نام (با = 'with') where نام means 'reputation/fame'; the final را again marks the whole noun phrase as definite/direct object. Word order is noun + modifiers + enclosing را, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s635-g75: درم is a monetary noun 'dirham' placed before the verb; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd person). ودینار is a fused poetic token 'and a dinar' (vdīnār) functioning as an additional direct object; the conjunction و links that to هرگونه چیز 'every kind of thing' — a free appositive/object phrase. The structure lists gifts as coordinated direct objects of داد.
segments.s635-g76: همان here functions as a demonstrative/namely ('the very' / 'namely'), introducing examples of the foregoing 'هرگونه چیز'. عنبر, عود are items of perfume/incense (here 'ambergris' and 'agarwood/oud'), each joined by و; کافورنیز is a fused poetic form meaning 'and camphor' (نیز emphasizing 'also'). The whole segment is a nominal list exemplifying the goods given.
segments.s635-g77: بیاراست is a poetic past-tense verb 'adorned' (from آراستن) and is used here in transitive sense. ایوان is the direct object 'iwan / portico' and گوهرنگار is a compound adjective 'jeweled' or 'gem-encrusted' modifying ایوان. The verb precedes its object in the poetic line but functions normally as 'he adorned the jeweled iwan.'
segments.s635-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; قنوج is the place-name Kannauj. هرکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever / each person who ...' The sequence بد نامدار is a two-word description: بد ordinarily means 'bad/ill' and نامدار 'renowned', so together they yield the idea 'ill-renowned' (notorious) or, in some poetic contexts, simply 'well-known/famous' depending on tone; grammatically this is an adjectival phrase modifying 'هرکس'.
segments.s635-g79: خرامان is an adverbial/participial adverb 'with a stately/proud gait' modifying the verb. بران is a deictic/poetic marker ('to that / thence') pointing to the place just mentioned; بزمگاه is the location 'banquet-hall' and آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past verb 'they came'. The clause describes the manner (خرامان) and place (بران بزمگاه) of their coming.
segments.s635-g80: به introduces a circumstantial phrase 'to/into' and شادی here means 'in celebration / with joy' (an adverbial use). همه 'all' is the subject/agent in plural and نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before / near'); آمدند repeats the 3rd-person plural past 'they came'. The clause states that everyone came, joyfully, into the king’s presence.
segments.s635-g17: 'بدو گفت فرزانه کای شهریار' is arranged as indirect object + verb + speaker + vocative + addressee: بدو (to him) comes before گفت (said); فرزانه names the speaker ('the sage'); کای is the vocative marker 'O' addressed to شهریار ('king'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' introducing the following quoted admonition.
segments.s635-g18: دلت را is دل + enclitic -ت (your heart) plus the object marker را emphasizing it; بدین‌گونه is an adverb 'in this manner/thus'; رنجه here functions as a verbal/adjectival idea 'distressed/suffering' and مدار is an archaic/poetic negative imperative meaning 'do not make/let (it be)'; the whole phrase means 'do not make your heart distressed in this way.'
segments.s635-g19: فرستاده is 'messenger' (subject/agent). شهریاران is genitive/plural 'of the kings' modifying فرستاده. کشی here is an agentive/relational element attached to شهریاران (lit. 'killing' / 'killer of'), creating an epithet; the exact nuance is elliptical/poetic and can mean something like 'connected with killing/killer' or form part of a compound epithet for the messenger.
segments.s635-g20: به marks direction/association ('with' / 'toward'); غمری is an adjective/noun 'sorrowful' describing the messenger or his mood; برد is the past verb 'bore/took' (here 'took' or 'set out along'); راه is the object 'the road/way'; و is the coordinating 'and'; بیدانشی (بی‌دانش‌ی) describes a quality 'ignorance/ignorant' and coordinates with غمری — the phrase says he 'took the road with sorrow and ignorance' or 'chose a sorrowful and ignorant course.' Note: some lexical entries provided above do not list برد in its verbal sense; here it functions as the past of بردن 'to take'.
segments.s635-g21: کس here is negative universal 'no one'; اندیشه زین‌گونه combines اندیشه 'thought' with the modifier زین‌گونه 'in this manner/like this'; هرگز نکرد is the negated past 'never did' (اندیشه کردن = 'to think'), so the clause means 'No one ever thought thus.'
segments.s635-g22: به راه suchی رای = 'on/to the path of such counsel' (به + راه marks following/adopting a course); چنین رای is 'such counsel/plan'; هرگز مگرد is an archaic/poetic negative imperative ('never turn / never go / never adopt'); the clause warns 'never follow such a plan.'
segments.s635-g23: بر + مهتران expresses the sphere/bench/among whom something is said ('among the chiefs/great ones'); زشت‌نامی بود is the predicate 'was ill‑famed/infamous' (زشت‌نامی = ill repute, بود = past copula), so 'he was infamous among the chiefs.'
segments.s635-g24: سپهبد is the subject/title 'spahbed' (a senior general); به مردم marks the social sphere 'to/among the people'; گرامی بود is the predicate 'was honored/esteemed' (گرامی = honored); together: 'the spahbed was dear/honored to the people.'
segments.s635-g41: Word-for-word order here is indirect object (بتو) → verb phrase (داد خواهم) → emphatic particle (همی) → object with possessive suffix (دخترم). داد خواهم is a periphrastic future: داد (give) + خواهم (I will), so “I will give.” همی is an older/poetic emphatic/continuous particle attached to the verb phrase. دخترم is the object with the enclitic -م “my.”
segments.s635-g42: ز introduces the standard poetic relation 'in/by' or 'with respect to'; گفتار و کردار is a coordinated noun phrase 'speech and conduct.' باشد is a jussive/subjunctive/present-form meaning 'may be/shall be' here expressing what is to obtain, and برم is the preposition بر plus the enclitic -م = 'to/for me' (i.e. 'be to me' or 'belong to me'). The whole unit is a purposive/result clause: 'let her be to me in word and deed.'
segments.s635-g43: چو این کرده باشم is a temporal/conditional clause: چو = 'when/if', این = 'this', کرده باشم = perfect-subjunctive/perfect auxiliary structure ('have done' with باشم used as auxiliary). بر من بایست is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'it is incumbent on me' or 'I must' (literally 'it stands upon me'). Word order places the temporal clause before the main obligation clause.
segments.s635-g44: کز = 'for/that' introducing the reason; ایـدر = 'here'; گذشتن = infinitive 'to pass/leave/go past'; ت + را represent the clitic combination ترا (you) marked by object marker را (here split in the list): ترا = 'you' as the logical argument of گذشتن. روی نیست is literal 'there is no face' — an idiom meaning no honor/standing to do so. The clause gives the reason: 'for from here you cannot go (with any face/honor).'
segments.s635-g45: ترا (ت + را) marks the second-person object 'you' (object clitic plus object marker). بر سپه = 'over the host/army' (بر = over/upon, سپه = army) and کامگاری is a nominal meaning 'success/victory.' دهم (understood in the clause) is the first-person singular present 'I give' (so the sense is 'I will give you success over the army'). Word order places the beneficiary first, then the domain of the grant, then the noun of result.
segments.s635-g46: به هندوستان شهریاری دهم = 'I will give rule/sovereignty in India.' به introduces the domain/location 'to/in India'; هندوستان is the place-name; شهریاری is 'sovereignty/rule.' The verb دهم ('I give') is the same first-person verb reused, yielding 'I will make you sovereign in India.' This is a simple ditransitive/benefactive construction.
segments.s635-g47: فروماند is an older/poetic past or resultative meaning 'fell low/was brought low/was cast down.' The personal name بهرام follows as the subject. اندیشه کرد is the common Persian compound 'did thinking' = 'he thought/pondered' (اندیشه noun + کرد auxiliary). The conjunction و simply links the clauses: 'Bahram fell and thought.'
segments.s635-g48: ز here means 'about/concerning' (poetic usage like 'from'); تخت و نژاد و ز ننگ و نبرد is a coordinated list of objects of thought: تخت = 'throne', نژاد = 'lineage', ننگ = 'shame', نبرد = 'battle/struggle'. The repeated ز before ننگ emphasizes the second pair; overall the phrase means he pondered matters of throne, descent, dishonor and war.
segments.s635-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. In older Persian the clause order can put the verb before the subject, so چو + بشنید شنگل = 'when Shangal heard'. سخن تیره شد is a separate predicative clause: سخن (subject) + تیره شد (predicate) = 'the speech/utterance became dark/gloomy' (i.e. his words or mood turned grim).
segments.s635-g34: ز گفتار فرزانگان is an ablative/cause phrase 'from / because of the words of the sages'. خیره شد is an adjectival copula: خیره (stunned/astounded) + شد 'became' = 'became stunned' (i.e. was struck dumb/astonished). The prepositional phrase precedes the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s635-g35: ببود is an archaic past copula 'it was' (old narrative past). آن شب و بامداد پگاه are temporal adverbials ('that night and at dawn/early morning') that set the time for the following action.
segments.s635-g36: فرستاد is the past transitive verb 'sent/dispatched'. کس is an indefinite object 'someone' and نزد + بهرامشاه marks the recipient/location 'to the presence of Bahram Shah'. The subject agent is not expressed here (typical in narrative), so the clause reads '[he/they] sent someone to Bahram Shah.'
segments.s635-g37: به introduces the circumstantial phrase 'to/in' the state described: به تنها تن خویش = 'in his solitary person' (i.e. 'by himself, alone'). خوی + ش together function here as the possessive 'خویش' ('his own'), and بی‌انجمن ('without assembly/counsel') further qualifies his solitary condition.
segments.s635-g38: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. دستور بد پیش is elliptical: دستور بد ('a bad order') + پیش used adverbially ('put forward/issued'), and رای زن = 'the counsel/opinion of a woman'. The verb is omitted (classical Persian often omits 'was/was taken'), so the sense is 'neither did he issue a bad command nor take a woman's counsel.'
segments.s635-g39: به + بهرام + گفت = 'said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object/recipient). The following ای دلارای مرد is a vocative address: ای 'O!' + دلارای 'beloved/precious' + مرد 'man/hero'. The verb گفت is the reported-speech past 'he said'.
segments.s635-g40: توانگر شدی is a 2nd-person past: 'you became rich/wealthy' (توانگر = wealthy; شدی = 'you became'). The rest is an admonition: گرد بیشی مگرد is archaic/condensed instruction meaning roughly 'do not become/exceed/turn into excess' — گرد and مگرد are archaic usages here, so the clause functions as a negative imperative 'do not be/exceed'.
segments.s635-g9: گر is the conditional particle 'if' introducing the protasis. از نزد ما is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from our presence' (نزد used in the sense 'in the presence of'); او is the third-person pronoun subject; به ایران شود places the destination 'to Iran' before the verb شود, an archaic/classical present/subjunctive-like form here meaning 'becomes / goes / comes (to be)'. Word order follows classical Persian where the verb ends the clause.
segments.s635-g10: به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'to/near' placed before the noun phrase شاه دلیران ('the king of the brave/valiant ones'); دلیران is the plural adjective used substantively ('the brave'), and شود again is the verb 'become / be brought' placed at the end. The clause states that he will come to a position near the heroic king.
segments.s635-g11: سپاه مرا is the possessed noun 'my army' (here split in the tokenization as م + را but functioning together as 'مرا' = 'my'). سست خواند is a past-tense reportative or simple past 'called/declared (it) weak' with the adjective سست as predicative; به کار means 'in action / for the task' (i.e. weak in action). The verb خواند comes after the object phrase, following classical word order.
segments.s635-g12: به هندوستان نیست is a verbless/nominal clause with نیست 'is not / there is not', here meaning 'there is none in Hindustan' or 'in India there is not'; classical Persian can use به + place with نیست for 'in (a place) there is not'. گوید سوار places the verb 'says' (گوید) before its subject سوار 'the rider', a common verb-subject order in poetry to preserve meter.
segments.s635-g13: سرافراز گردد uses the adjective سرافراز 'triumphant/exalted' with گردد (archaic subjunctive/passive-like 'become'), forming 'become triumphant'. مگَر here functions as a rhetorical interrogative particle ('could it be that.../shall it be that...?') rather than a simple 'unless', and دشمنم is 'my enemy' (دشمن + enclitic possessive ـم). The sequence poses a rhetorical question about the enemy's exaltation.
segments.s635-g14: فرستاده را is the definite object 'the messenger' marked by را; سر ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'the head from the body' (ز = 'from' in poetic/classical usage); برکنم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive/future-form of the verb برکندن 'to pluck off / remove', here idiomatically 'to behead'. The verb comes at the end, expressing the speaker's asserted action.
segments.s635-g15: نهانش is نهان 'secret/hidden' with the enclitic pronoun ـش 'him', meaning 'him secretly' or 'secretly him'; همی is the archaic continuous/progressive particle that marks ongoing or emphatic action; کرد is the light verb 'did/made' used with the following verbal/adjectival complement; خواهم is the first-person auxiliary/modal 'I will / I desire' and تباه is the adjective 'ruined/corrupt'. Together they express an emphatic future: 'I will secretly cause his ruin.'
segments.s635-g16: چه is an interrogative 'what/how' introducing the question; بینید is second-person-plural present/subjunctive/indicative 'you see' and این را is the object 'this' (marked by را). The next clause چه دانید راه is parallel: چه 'what' + دانید 'do you know' + راه 'the way/path' (object without an explicit را). Both clauses are rhetorical questions: 'What do you see in this? What do you know of the way?'
segments.s635-g49: ابا here is a verb meaning 'to hesitate / to be reluctant'; it appears before خویشتن + گفت to mean 'he hesitated and said to himself'. خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'himself'. کاین = که + این is the subordinating particle 'that/this', introducing the content of what he said; جنگ نیست is a simple nominal phrase negated by نیست ('is not').
segments.s635-g50: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/on account of' introducing the cause; پیوند شنگل is a noun phrase 'the bond/tie of Shangal' (شنگل is an opaque proper/name-like term); مرا را corresponds to the object 'me' (written here as م + را) and marks the person affected; ننگ نیست is 'there is no shame' (ننگ 'shame', نیست 'is not'). The whole reads 'I have no shame on account of the tie to Shangal.'
segments.s635-g51: و دیگر is a connective 'and besides/also'; که introduces the subordinate clause. جان بر سر آرم is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'I place/stake my life' (جان 'life' + بر سر 'on the head/of' + آرم, first-person verb), with آرم here as a first-person verb form expressing the speaker's volition. بدین is a deictic 'for/by this' (ب- prefix + دین in older/poetic usage) linking the clause to what follows.
segments.s635-g52: ببینم is first-person optative/indicative 'let me see / I will see'. مگر here functions as a particle meaning 'perhaps / whether' (introducing an expectation or question). خاک ایران‌زمین is the object 'the soil/land of Iran' with the compound noun ایران‌زمین.
segments.s635-g53: که introduces a conditional/explanatory clause. ایدر بدین‌سان is an adverbial phrase 'here in this manner'. بماندیم is the (past/optative) form 'we remain / we should remain' describing the hypothetical duration; دیر is an adverb meaning 'long' (of time) and specifies the duration of staying.
segments.s635-g54: برآویخت is a past tense verb '(would) spring up / rose up'. با is the instrumental preposition 'with'; دام روباه is a noun phrase 'the fox's trap' (دام 'trap' modified by روباه). شیر ('lion') names the agent and is placed after the instrument here for poetic inversion: 'a lion (sh) sprang up with the fox's trap.'
segments.s635-g55: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted poetic order 'thus gave (an) answer' (چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces reported speech or the content of the reply. فرمان کنم uses فرمان as a verbal noun 'command/order' with کنم (subjunctive/volitional 'I will do') to mean 'I will issue a command.'
segments.s635-g56: ز here is the preposition 'because of / from' and گفتارت is the genitive 'your speech/words' (گفتار + enclitic -ت). آرایش جان کنم is a verb phrase meaning 'I will make (آرایش) my life (جان) [an adornment]' with کنم showing volition; together it expresses 'I will, because of your words, make my life an ornament' (i.e., devote or risk my life).
segments.s635-g57: "تو" is the explicit 2nd-person subject ('you') followed by the prepositional phrase "از هر سه دختر" ('out of the three daughters'); Persian order here is preposition + noun phrase. "یکی" is the selected item/object ('one'), and "برگزین" is an imperative verb (2nd-person singular) meaning 'choose' — an exhortation addressed to 'تو'.
segments.s635-g58: The clause begins with the subordinating/relative particle "که" linking this result/clause to the previous imperative (here functioning roughly 'for/that' or 'and when'). "چون" is used in a temporal/simile sense ('when' / 'as'), followed by the 1st-person present form with enclitic object "بینمش" ('I see her' — here used as 'when I see her'). "خوانمش" is a 1st-person singular verb + object clitic meaning 'I call/name her' (not 'read'), and the bare word "آفرین" is the name given — here used as a proper name. The clause as a whole: 'that when I see her I will call her Afarin.'
segments.s635-g59: "ز" (poetic variant of "از") introduces the cause ('from/because of') followed by "گفتار او" ('her speech/words'). "شاد شد" is the compound of adjective + past auxiliary meaning 'became happy' (past tense). "شاه هند" is the subject 'the King of Hind' (title + place-name). Note the causal fronting: cause phrase + result verb + subject in poetic word order.
segments.s635-g60: "بیاراست" here functions as a past/action verb 'adorned' (poetic past form); the direct object "ایوان" ('the iwan/porch') follows it in this inverted, poetically compressed order. "به چینی پرند" is an instrumental/ornamental phrase 'with Chinese birds' ("به" marking the means/material, "چینی" qualifying the birds). The whole segment: 'he adorned the iwan with Chinese birds.'
segments.s635-g61: سه دختر is a quantified noun phrase ('three maidens') where the numeral precedes the noun. بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' placed after the subject (typical Persian word order: Subject ... Verb). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and خرم بهار is the simile's head 'joyful (خرم) spring (بهار)'. Note the compact, poetic order: numeral + noun + verb, then the simile introduced by چو.
segments.s635-g62: به begins an adverbial phrase indicating manner ('in/with'—here marking the way they came). A coordinated series of nouns follows (آرایش و بوی و رنگ و نگار), linked by و 'and' and not repeated with separate prepositions: this is a poetic accumulation listing their adornment, scent, color and beauty. The whole phrase modifies بیامد (tells how they came).
segments.s635-g63: به بهرام گور is a prepositional phrase 'to Bahram Gūr' (گور here is the epithet 'Gūr'/'Onager' attached to the name). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time' placed before the verb. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing reported/direct speech, and رو here functions as the object/command word 'face' (understood as 'turn your face' in the ensuing injunction). Word order shows topical fronting of the addressee and time before the verb.
segments.s635-g64: بیارای is a poetic/imperative form meaning 'adorn' or 'make ready' directed to the addressee; it governs the direct object دل را ('the heart' with the object marker را). The prepositional phrase به دیدار نو ('for a new/fresh sight/meeting') gives the purpose or occasion. In sum, this is an imperative clause: 'adorn the heart for a new sight,' with را marking دل as the definite object.
segments.s635-g65: بشد is the past/copulative form meaning “became” or “turned” used here to mean ‘he moved/quickened’ (classical/poetic past). تیز is an adjectival/predicative element ‘swift/quick’; the order (verb + predicate + subject) is typical in older Persian poetry: literally ‘became swift Bahram’. و links the action to the next clause. او را بدید is a straightforward past transitive clause: او is the pronoun ‘he/she’, را is the definite/direct‑object marker placed after the pronoun, and بدید is the past tense ‘saw’ (the b‑prefixed past common in classical Persian).
segments.s635-g66: ازان is an older contracted form meaning ‘of those / from those’ (≈ از آن). ماه‌رویان is the plural noun ‘moon‑faced (ones)’ = beautiful faces; یکی is the indefinite/partitive ‘one’ (a single one of them) placed before the verb, and برگزید is the past transitive ‘chose/selected’. Word order places the chosen item before the verb, a common poetic syntax.
segments.s635-g67: چو here introduces a simile ‘as / like’. خرم بهاری is a descriptive phrase ‘joyful/cheerful as spring’ (خرم = joyful; بهاری = spring‑like). سپینود نام is an appositive ‘Sepinud by name’ with the name following نام in the poetic word order (name after the noun).
segments.s635-g68: This is a piled list of qualities: همه (all/entirely) modifies the following nouns. شرم here carries the sense ‘modesty’ (not just shame), ناز means ‘coquetry/coyness’, رای is ‘counsel/plan’, and کام is ‘desire/wish’ (often erotic or longing). The repeated use of همه emphasizes completeness: she embodied all modesty and coyness, and every counsel and desire.
segments.s635-g69: بدو is the indirect object pronoun ‘to her/to that one’ (to + demonstrative in older usage). داد is the past tense ‘gave’. شنگل names the item given (an obscure/poetic object), and سپینود را repeats/identifies the recipient with the object‑marker را (common in poetry for emphasis or clarity). The sequence thus means ‘he gave the shangal to Sepinud’ (ﻋﻤﻮًn indirect object before or together with the marked name).
segments.s635-g70: چو again introduces similes: سرو سهی is a compound image ‘a cypress (tall beauty), dark/black (سهی = dark/black)’ — a conventional poetic epithet for a tall, dark‑haired beauty. شمع بی‌دود (literally ‘a candle without smoke’) is another flattering image ‘a pure/smokeless candle’; را here links the simile back to the thing/person being likened. These are pictorial appositions describing Sepinud’s appearance.
segments.s635-g71: یکی is an indefinite ‘one / a certain one’ chosen here; گنج is ‘treasure’, and پرمایه‌تر is the comparative ‘more substantial / richer’ (i.e., ‘richer in treasure’). برگزید is the past verb ‘chose/selected’. The noun phrase ‘یکی گنج پرمایه‌تر’ describes the person chosen before the verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s635-g72: بدان is the archaic contracted form meaning ‘to that’ (equivalent to به + آن) introducing the indirect object. ماه‌رخ ‘moon‑faced’ names/describes the recipient. داد is ‘gave’ (past). شنگل کلید is a possessive/nominal phrase ‘the key of the shangal’ (شنگل = the object/treasure previously mentioned; کلید = key). Word order places the indirect object phrase before the verb and the thing given after the verb.
segments.s635-g25: پس‌انگه is an adverbial particle meaning 'then/afterwards' and sets the temporal frame. بیاید here is a poetic/narrative verb form of آمدن placed before its phrase (inversion common in epic Persian) and functions as the main verb 'came'; از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' modifying the origin of سپاه, and سپاه (army) is the logical subject/agent even though it follows the verb and prepositional phrase due to poetic word order.
segments.s635-g26: یکی modifies تاجداری as 'a certain' or 'one' (i.e. 'a certain crown-bearer/sovereign'). تاجداری is a deverbal/noun phrase meaning kingship or the crowned ruler; چو is the comparative 'like/as', and بهرامشاه is the proper name being compared to. The whole noun phrase is appositive to the previous clause (identifying someone among the arriving force).
segments.s635-g27: نماند is the negative past/existential 'did not remain' (here functioning as 'there remained not'). ز ما کس is an ablative/partitive phrase 'anyone from us' (with the negation making it 'no one of us'). بدینجا is the locative/directional 'to/for this place', and درست is a predicate adjective 'fit/right/proper' describing suitability for that place — word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s635-g28: ز نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'out of kindness' (ز = 'because of/from'). نباید is the impersonal modal 'one must not/should not'. ترا (ت + را) is the 2nd-person object 'you' (ت = 2nd-person suffix, را = object marker). دست شست literally 'to wash the hand(s)' is an idiom meaning 'to wash one's hands of (someone)' i.e. to abandon or disclaim responsibility; here the statement forbids abandoning you out of goodwill.
segments.s635-g29: رهانیده is a past participle/prefective form 'freed/delivered'. ما is the object 'us' and ست (a poetic form of است) is the copula/auxiliary, so the clause conveys 'he has freed us' or 'it is that he freed us'. از اژدها is the source 'from the dragon'. The structure combines participle + object + copula, a common classical pattern to express perfective result.
segments.s635-g30: نه is the negation 'not'. کشتن is the verbal noun 'killing'. بود is the past copula 'was', linking the subject (کشتن) to the predicate. رنج او را بها is a genitive-object phrase 'the price (بها) of his (او) suffering (رنج)' with را marking the object; overall: 'Killing was not the price of his suffering.'
segments.s635-g31: بدین here is the locative 'in/on this' (in the data it was split into ب + دین; ب is the prefix). بوم ما is 'our homeland/soil'. اژدها کشت is 'the dragon killed' (کشت used here in the sense 'killed'). و is the conjunction 'and'. کرگ is an archaic/dialectal word (contextual meaning uncertain; it can mean 'down/nap' or a type of bird) and the exact sense in the epic is ambiguous; the clause lists what the dragon did on the land.
segments.s635-g32: به تن is the dative/benefactive 'to the body' or 'upon the body'. زندگانی فزایش is a noun phrase 'an increase of life' (زَندگی + فزایش), placed before the contrastive نه مرگ 'not death' — a poetic inversion that emphasizes life rather than death. Grammatically this is a verbless predicative clause: '(there was) to the body an increase of life, not death.'
segments.s635-g1: همان before شاه is a demonstrative/adjective: 'that/the same king'. شنگل is an epithet or name in apposition to شاه. دلی پر ز درد is a compact phrase: دلی (a heart) + پر 'full (of)' + ز (poetic 'from/of') + درد 'pain/sorrow' — literally 'a heart full of sorrow.' Word order is noun + adjective + prepositional source.
segments.s635-g2: همی داشت is a classical periphrasis indicating continuous/repeated past action ('was having' / 'used to have'); the subject (the king) is omitted but carried over from the previous phrase. از here has a causal sense 'because of' and takes کار او ('his affair/deed') as its object. روی زرد is a predicate/object phrase 'a pale face' (روی 'face' + adjective زرد 'pale'); the negative/causal phrase is inserted before that predicate for poetic word order.
segments.s635-g3: شب آمد (night came) is a simple past clause; بیاورد follows آمد in a common epic construction where آمد + present/subjunctive verb conveys 'came bringing' or 'came and brought.' فرزانه را is the definite object marked by را. The subject is omitted (poetic) and understood from context.
segments.s635-g4: همان مردم is 'those same people.' خوی + ش here represents the possessive 'خویش' split in the tokenization: خوی 'kin/own' + ش (the enclitic possessive 'his/her') → 'his own.' بیگانه 'strangers' is coordinated with خویش by و. The final را again marks the whole phrase as an object (parallel to فرزانه را).
segments.s635-g5: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus (he) said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت past 'said'). کاین is the archaic fused relative/demonstrative (often equivalent to 'که این' → 'that this/that'), here introducing 'that man'; مرد is used in the heroic sense 'man/hero' and بهرامشاه is an appositive proper name identifying him.
segments.s635-g6: بدین is the classical compound 'به + این' = 'by/with this' (the tokenization shows it as ب + دین, but functionally بدین = 'with this'). It governs زور 'strength' (بدین زور = 'with this strength'). The following وَ phrases repeat این 'this' for parallel items: 'and this horn and this دستگاه (apparatus/establishment)'. Note: دین in the token list does not carry its literal sense here (it is part of بدین), so its standalone senses do not apply.
segments.s635-g7: نباشد is a negative jussive/subjunctive form meaning 'may there not be' / 'let there not be' (a classical negative optative). همی functions as an emphatic/adverbial 'at all/ever' here. ایدر means 'here'. از هیچ روی is an idiomatic expression 'from no aspect / in no respect' where از introduces the scope and هیچ 'no/none' negates it.
segments.s635-g8: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of.' هرگونه 'of any kind' modifies آمیختم, which here functions as a nominalized/past participle sense 'mixing' or 'that which is mixed' (the lexical entry lists 'mixed' / 'mixing'). رنگ و بوی are the coordinated nouns 'color and smell/scent' and form the thing that must not be mixed; the whole phrase means 'no mixing of any kind of color and scent.'

sentences.s635-s41: They stayed a week with wine in hand; everyone sat down happy and joyful in their places.
sentences.s635-s42: Sepinud was with King Bahram Gūr, while the wine shone bright in crystal cups.
sentences.s635-s37: He brought Bahram’s companions — the riders who had returned, famed.
sentences.s635-s38: He gave dirhams and dinars and every kind of thing — namely ambergris, oud, and also camphor.
sentences.s635-s39: He adorned the jewel-studded iwan; from Kannauj whoever was well-known (or notorious) —
sentences.s635-s40: They came proudly to that banquet-hall; all, in joy, came before the king.
sentences.s635-s9: The sage said to him, "O king, do not distress your heart in this way."
sentences.s635-s10: He sent a messenger (a man) of the kings; (the messenger) took the road with sorrow and ignorance.
sentences.s635-s11: No one ever thought in this way; never follow such counsel on the road.
sentences.s635-s12: The spahbed was infamous among the chiefs, but he was honored by the people.
sentences.s635-s21: I will give you my daughter; let her be mine in word and deed.
sentences.s635-s22: When I have done this I must stand by it, for you cannot leave here with any face (i.e. without shame).
sentences.s635-s23: I will grant you victory over the host, and I will give you sovereignty in India.
sentences.s635-s24: Bahram sank down and pondered, considering the throne and lineage and the shame and the fight.
sentences.s635-s17: When Shangal heard, his speech turned dark; he was struck dumb by the words of the sages.
sentences.s635-s18: That night and at the break of dawn someone was sent to Bahram Shah.
sentences.s635-s19: Alone, by his solitary self and without counsel, neither did he give a bad order nor accept a woman's advice.
sentences.s635-s20: He said to Bahram, 'O beloved man: you have become wealthy; do not grow presumptuous/excessive.'
sentences.s635-s5: If he comes from our presence into Iran, he will be brought near the king of the brave.
sentences.s635-s6: He called my army weak in action; the rider says there is none in Hindustan.
sentences.s635-s7: Shall my enemy be exalted? — unless I cut the envoy's head from his body.
sentences.s635-s8: I will secretly bring about his ruin; what do you see in this, what do you know of the way?
sentences.s635-s25: He muttered to himself, hesitating, that this is not a battle; I have no shame because of my tie to Shangal.
sentences.s635-s26: And moreover — that I should stake my life on this — let me see, perhaps, the soil of the land of Iran.
sentences.s635-s27: For if we were to remain here in this manner for long, a lion would spring up with the fox's trap.
sentences.s635-s28: He answered thus: 'I will give the order; because of your words I will make my life an ornament.'
sentences.s635-s29: You choose one of the three daughters; when I see her I will name her "Afarin."
sentences.s635-s30: The King of Hind became joyful at her words and adorned the iwan with Chinese birds.
sentences.s635-s31: Three maidens came like the joyful spring, arrayed in adornment, scent, color and grace.
sentences.s635-s32: At that time someone said to Bahram Gūr, 'Turn (your) face; adorn your heart for a fresh sight.'
sentences.s635-s33: Bahram quickened his pace, saw her, and chose one of those moon‑faced women.
sentences.s635-s34: Fair Sepinud — joyous as the spring — was all modesty and coyness, and all counsel and desire.
sentences.s635-s35: To her he gave the shangal, Sepinud (—) like a dark cypress, like a smokeless candle.
sentences.s635-s36: He chose one richer in treasure, and to that moon‑faced one he gave the key of the shangal.
sentences.s635-s13: Then an army came from Iran — a certain crowned ruler like Bahram-shah.
sentences.s635-s14: No one of us remained fit for this place; out of kindness one should not wash one's hands of you.
sentences.s635-s15: He has freed us from the dragon; killing was not the price of his suffering.
sentences.s635-s16: On this our soil the dragon killed (and) the bird — to the body an increase of life, not death.
sentences.s635-s1: That same King Shangel had a heart full of sorrow, and his face was pale because of that affair.
sentences.s635-s2: Night came and brought the sage, and those same people — his own and strangers.
sentences.s635-s3: Thus he said: 'That man, Bahram-shah, with this strength and this horn and this apparatus...'
sentences.s635-s4: Let there be here, in no respect, any mixing of color and scent of any kind.
